title: Stop screeching about immigration, and get smart about it
gist: After SCOTUS reaffirmed birthright citizenship under the 14th Amendment, Noah Smith argues the loud rightist backlash (Sean Davis, Matt Walsh) represents a small, screechy minority against a supermajority that supports both birthright citizenship and immigration generally, and that Trump-era immigration cuts are worsening America's fertility-driven demographic aging — making skewing legal immigration toward college-educated workers the fiscally optimal, and politically popular, path forward.

themes.theme: Birthright citizenship ruling and the reaction

insights.claim: The Supreme Court reaffirmed that the 14th Amendment grants automatic birthright citizenship to anyone born in the U.S., including children of illegal immigrants and temporary visa holders, rejecting the Trump administration's attempt to exclude both groups via executive order.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Closes off executive-order-based rollback of birthright citizenship as a legal avenue, meaning any future restriction would require a constitutional amendment — a much higher political bar.
insights.locator: Birthright citizenship is the law of the land

insights.claim: Prominent right-wing commentators Sean Davis (The Federalist) and Matt Walsh reacted to the birthright-citizenship ruling with extreme rhetoric — floating dissolving the Union or sterilizing foreign visitors — which Smith characterizes as a 'screechy online fringe' rather than representative of majority Republican opinion.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Signals that maximalist anti-immigration rhetoric is a minority, performative position rather than a broad electoral mandate, which matters for gauging how much political risk premium to assign to nativist policy threats.

insights.claim: Nearly 70% of respondents in a Quinnipiac University poll (fielded June 18-22) said the Supreme Court should keep birthright citizenship in place, versus just 27% who wanted it reversed.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Quantifies the supermajority backing birthright citizenship, undercutting the credibility of the vocal rightist backlash as a mandate for further legal challenges.
insights.quote: Nearly 70 percent of respondents in a Quinnipiac University poll think the Supreme Court should keep birthright citizenship in place.
insights.locator: Here's an example from The Hill

insights.claim: 79% of Americans say immigration is good for the country overall, even while acknowledging it will cause long-term cultural change — making the 'cultural preservation' argument against immigration a lost cause numerically, regardless of how loudly its proponents object.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Establishes that pro-immigration sentiment is a broad, stable American majority position, not a fringe or elite-only view — relevant to handicapping the durability of restrictionist policy shifts.
insights.quote: 79% of Americans say that immigration is good for the country overall
insights.locator: MAGA has already lost that battle

insights.claim: About half of Americans, per Pew polling, think birthright citizenship specifically should be denied to children of illegal immigrants, showing a more nuanced public view: broadly pro-immigration overall, but split on this narrower legal question.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Shows the electorate distinguishes between illegal-immigration enforcement (where views are split) and immigration in general (where support is strong) — a nuance that a blanket pro- or anti-immigration platform would misread.
insights.locator: That doesn't mean the public entirely disagrees

insights.claim: Within the MAGA movement, taking a maximalist anti-immigration stance functions as a loyalty signal, while supporting pragmatic, winnable compromises signals one is not a core part of the movement — which blocks the kind of concerted campaign that could actually secure the votes for a constitutional amendment narrowly targeting illegal immigrants' birthright citizenship.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Explains why the anti-immigration coalition keeps choosing maximalist rhetoric over an achievable narrower legal win — a structural reason to expect continued legislative gridlock rather than policy change on this issue.
insights.quote: Taking a maximalist position on the issue is a way for people to signal their allegiance to the MAGA movement
insights.locator: So if the MAGA movement were pragmatic

insights.claim: U.S. fertility, which was near replacement level roughly two decades ago, has now fallen below the level Japan had in the 1980s, putting America on Japan's aging-and-shrinking population trajectory unless offset by immigration.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Establishes the scale of the demographic problem immigration is being asked to offset — without inflows, the U.S. is on track to replicate Japan's multi-decade aging/shrinkage story.
insights.locator: The U.S. is suffering a fertility crisis

insights.claim: Per Duzhak (2025) estimates, Trump's restrictive immigration policies — both deportations and sharp cuts to legal immigration — have almost entirely cut off the immigrant inflow in 2025 and 2026, with some estimates showing the immigrant population actually declining.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Quantifies a concrete, current policy-driven shock to labor-force and population growth inputs, directly relevant to forecasting U.S. potential GDP growth and labor supply.

insights.claim: With zero immigration, the number of working-age Americans supporting each retiree will fall from 3 to 2 over the next quarter century, per Lant Pritchett, directly raising the tax and eldercare burden on the young and middle-aged.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Quantifies a specific labor-support-ratio deterioration that translates into higher elder-care taxes and labor costs — a macro headwind for growth and margins if immigration stays curtailed.
insights.quote: the number of American working-age people per retiree will fall from 3 to 2
insights.locator: For one thing, it means far fewer workers

insights.claim: Immigrants are disproportionately likely to work in jobs caring for the elderly, per Paul Krugman citing Migration Policy Institute data, so cutting immigration simultaneously shrinks the population that funds retiree support and the specific labor pool that provides eldercare.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Identifies a double-hit mechanism from immigration restriction: fewer workers paying in AND fewer caregivers available, compounding the eldercare cost/labor squeeze.

insights.claim: The rising burden of caring for aging parents will likely suppress fertility further, since it is difficult to support both children and retired parents simultaneously — creating a compounding demographic doom loop as immigration is restricted, with small towns and rural areas hit hardest.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Identifies a feedback loop where today's immigration cuts could depress tomorrow's birth rate too, compounding the long-run demographic and fiscal problem rather than just adding a one-time shock.
insights.quote: it’s hard supporting kids and your retired parents at the same time!
insights.locator: The burden of supporting the elderly

insights.claim: Fiscal-impact data from Daniel DiMartino shows college-educated immigrants tend to decrease the national debt while immigrants without college degrees tend to add to it, since lower-education immigrants earn less and draw more on government benefits for themselves and their children.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Shows the fiscal case for immigration hinges on skill/education composition rather than raw volume — a concrete filter for evaluating which immigration reforms would actually help the federal balance sheet.
insights.locator: If you just look at immigrants themselves

insights.claim: Smith's own generation-inclusive analysis finds that, factoring in later generations' strong upward mobility, the long-term fiscal impact of immigration is more positive overall, but a large gap by original immigrant education level persists — leading him to prescribe more legal skilled immigration, more border security, and less quasi-legal asylum, which he notes matches what polling says Americans actually want.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Offers a concrete, investable policy prescription — skewing legal immigration toward college-educated workers is the lever that turns immigration from a fiscal drag into a net positive, relevant to labor-supply-dependent sectors like healthcare, tech, and eldercare.
insights.quote: more legal skilled immigration, more border security, and less quasi-legal asylum grants
insights.locator: So if we want to use immigration as a tool

insights.claim: A backlash against the disorderly, quasi-legal immigration surge under Biden's lax border enforcement probably helped elect Donald Trump in 2024, and Smith warns that if Democrats now respond to MAGA nativism by embracing open borders, voters will keep swinging back toward electing immigration hardliners.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Warns against a predictable political overcorrection cycle — lax-border Democratic policy begets hardline Republican backlash — that keeps U.S. immigration policy oscillating rather than converging on the skilled-immigration approach Smith argues is optimal.
insights.quote: A backlash against the disorderly flood of quasi-legal immigration under Biden helped get Trump elected in 2024.
insights.locator: A backlash against the disorderly flood
